items.title: Use plain language and avoid figures of speech, idioms, and complicated metaphors.
items.description: Write content at an 8th grade reading level.
items.whyItMatters: Plain language lowers cognitive load for users with cognitive disabilities, non-native readers, and stressed users.
items.bad: It is a no-brainer that we will move the needle and circle back once we have boiled the ocean on this paradigm-shifting deliverable.
items.good: This is a simple choice. We will work on it together and update you next week.

items.title: Make sure that button, a, and label element content is unique and descriptive.
items.description: Terms like "click here" and "read more" do not provide any context. Some people navigate using a list of all links on a page.
items.whyItMatters: Screen reader users who navigate by links hear link text out of context — "click here" gives no useful information.

items.title: Use left-aligned text for left-to-right (LTR) languages, and right-aligned text for right-to-left (RTL) languages.
items.description: Centered-aligned or justified text is difficult to read.
items.whyItMatters: Justified text creates uneven word spacing that makes reading harder, especially for users with dyslexia.

items.title: Validate your HTML.
items.description: Valid HTML helps to provide a consistent, expected experience across all browsers and assistive technology.
items.whyItMatters: Malformed HTML can confuse screen readers and cause unpredictable behavior in assistive technologies.

items.title: Use a lang attribute on the html element.
items.description: This helps assistive technology such as screen readers to pronounce content correctly.
items.whyItMatters: Without a lang attribute, screen readers may use the wrong language pronunciation engine, making content unintelligible.

items.title: Provide a unique title for each page or view.
items.description: The title element is often the first piece of information announced by assistive technology.
items.whyItMatters: Unique page titles help users understand where they are, especially when switching between multiple browser tabs.

items.title: Ensure that viewport zoom is not disabled.
items.description: Some people need to increase the size of text to a point where they can read it.
items.whyItMatters: Disabling zoom prevents low-vision users from being able to read your content at all.

items.title: Use landmark elements to indicate important content regions.
items.description: Landmark regions help communicate the layout and important areas of a page or view.
items.whyItMatters: Screen reader users rely on landmark regions to skip directly to navigation, main content, or other sections.

items.title: Avoid using positive tabindex attribute values.
items.description: Having an illogical tab order makes navigation confusing for those using assistive technology.
items.whyItMatters: Positive tabindex values create jumbled keyboard navigation order that confuses keyboard-only and screen reader users.

items.title: Avoid using the autofocus attribute.
items.description: People who are blind or who have low vision may be disoriented when focus is moved without their permission.
items.whyItMatters: Autofocus disrupts screen reader users who may be mid-sentence when focus is forcibly stolen.

items.title: Allow extending session timeouts.
items.description: Let the person using your site easily turn off, adjust, or extend their session well before it ends.
items.whyItMatters: Users with cognitive or motor disabilities need extra time and will lose data if sessions expire without warning.
items.bad: Session expired. Please log in again.

items.title: Remove title attribute tooltips.
items.description: The title attribute has numerous issues and should not be used if the information is important for all people to access.
items.whyItMatters: title tooltips are inaccessible on touch screens, inconsistent across screen readers, and invisible to keyboard-only users.

items.title: Make sure there is a visible focus style for interactive elements navigated via keyboard.
items.description: Let your focus styles flow from your brand. For example, if you use rounded buttons, make your focus ring rounded as well.
items.whyItMatters: Keyboard-only users rely entirely on the visible focus indicator to know which element is currently active.

items.title: Check to see that keyboard focus order matches the visual layout.
items.description: Check to see that keyboard focus order matches the visual layout. Use the tab key to navigate.
items.whyItMatters: Mismatched focus order causes keyboard users to jump unpredictably across the page, losing their place and context.
items.good: /* DOM order matches visual order — no order property mismatches */

items.title: Remove invisible focusable elements.
items.description: Remove the ability to focus on elements that are not presently meant to be discoverable.
items.whyItMatters: Invisible focusable elements create ghost tab stops that confuse keyboard users who cannot see where focus went.

items.title: Make sure that all img elements have an alt attribute.
items.description: Alternate text provides a textual alternative to non-text content in web pages.
items.whyItMatters: Without alt text, screen readers announce the raw filename which is meaningless or confusing to users.

items.title: Make sure that decorative images use null alt (empty) attribute values.
items.description: Decorative images that don't add informational value should be hidden from assistive technology.
items.whyItMatters: Decorative images with descriptive alt text create noise for screen reader users trying to understand content.

items.title: Provide a text alternative for complex images such as charts, graphs, and maps.
items.description: Is the information presented in the chart, graph, or map also available in text format?
items.whyItMatters: Screen readers cannot interpret visual data — a text alternative ensures no information is lost for blind users.

items.title: For images containing text, make sure the alt description includes the image's text.
items.description: For example, the FedEx logo should have an alt value of "FedEx."
items.whyItMatters: If text is embedded in an image, it must be in the alt text so blind users receive the same information.

items.title: Use heading elements to introduce content.
items.description: Do not use a heading element for a subheading, title, or tagline that is not introducing a section of content.
items.whyItMatters: Semantic headings let screen reader users navigate page structure — styled paragraphs are completely invisible to them.

items.title: Use only one h1 element per page or view.
items.description: The h1 element should describe the page or view a person is on.
items.whyItMatters: Multiple h1 elements confuse the document outline, making it harder for screen reader users to understand page structure.

items.title: Heading elements should be written in a logical sequence.
items.description: Heading hierarchy should only increase by one level at a time.
items.whyItMatters: Out-of-order headings break the logical document outline that screen reader users navigate by.

items.title: Don't skip heading levels.
items.description: Skipping heading levels can cause confusion when navigating with a screen reader.
items.whyItMatters: Skipped heading levels break the expected document outline and cause confusion for AT navigation.

items.title: Use list elements (ol, ul, and dl elements) for list content.
items.description: Using list elements communicates to assistive technology that content is a list.
items.whyItMatters: Semantic lists tell screen readers how many items are in the list, enabling efficient navigation.

items.title: Use the a element for links.
items.description: Links created by the a element have functionality that is expected by a person using assistive technology.
items.whyItMatters: Only real <a> elements appear in screen reader link lists, are bookmarkable, and open correctly with middle-click.

items.title: Ensure that links are recognizable as links.
items.description: Identify links with more than just color to ensure that people who cannot distinguish colors can navigate.
items.whyItMatters: Color alone is insufficient — colorblind users and high-contrast mode users may not see color differences.
items.bad: a { color: blue; /* only color, no underline */ }

items.title: Ensure that controls have :focus states.
items.description: Do not suppress CSS outline styles without providing an alternative focus style.
items.whyItMatters: Without visible focus states, keyboard users cannot tell which interactive element is currently active.

items.title: Use the button element for buttons.
items.description: Buttons created by the button element have functionality that is expected by a person using assistive technology.
items.whyItMatters: Only real <button> elements activate with Space/Enter, are in tab order by default, and are announced as buttons by screen readers.

items.title: Provide a skip link and make sure that it is visible when focused.
items.description: A skip link can be used to provide quick access to a single portion of the document.
items.whyItMatters: Without a skip link, keyboard users must Tab through every navigation item on every page load before reaching content.
items.bad: <!-- No skip link — user must tab through all navigation -->
items.good: Skip to main content

items.title: Identify links that open in a new tab or window.
items.description: While this technique is not required for compliance, it is an often-cited area of frustration for many assistive technology users.
items.whyItMatters: Unexpectedly opening a new tab disorients screen reader users and breaks the back-button navigation flow.
items.good: Read more (opens in new tab)

items.title: Use the table element to describe tabular data.
items.description: Do any tables exist? If so, are they marked up semantically to describe the table data?
items.whyItMatters: CSS grid divs have no table semantics — screen readers cannot navigate by row/column or announce cell relationships.

items.title: Use the th element for table headers (with appropriate scope attributes).
items.description: Scope attributes on th elements improve support in some older browsers.
items.whyItMatters: Without scope attributes, screen readers may not correctly associate header cells with their data cells.

items.title: Use the caption element to provide a title for the table.
items.description: The caption element identifies the overall topic of a table and is useful in most circumstances.
items.whyItMatters: Screen reader users hear the caption before navigating a table, giving crucial context about its contents.

items.title: All inputs in a form are associated with a corresponding label element.
items.description: Use a for/id pairing to guarantee the highest level of browser/assistive technology support.
items.whyItMatters: Without associated labels, screen readers only announce "edit text" when a user focuses an input — no purpose is communicated.

items.title: Use fieldset and legend elements where appropriate.
items.description: Does your form contain multiple inputs that relate to the same topic? Use fieldset to group them.
items.whyItMatters: Without fieldset/legend, screen reader users hear radio button labels without knowing what group they belong to.
items.bad:
Shipping speed

Standard Express
items.good:
<fieldset>
  <legend>Shipping speed</legend>
  <input type="radio" name="s" id="std"><label for="std">Standard</label>
  <input type="radio" name="s" id="exp"><label for="exp">Express</label>
</fieldset>

items.title: Inputs use autocomplete where appropriate.
items.description: Inputs that collect information about a person should use appropriate autocomplete values.
items.whyItMatters: Autocomplete tokens let browsers and AT auto-fill forms for users with cognitive or motor disabilities.

items.title: Make sure that form input errors are displayed in list above the form after submission.
items.description: Providing a list of errors at the top of the form helps with recognition, context, and navigation.
items.whyItMatters: Inline-only errors can be missed by screen reader users who have already moved past the field.
items.good:
2 errors found:

  • Email is required

items.title: Associate input error messaging with the input it corresponds to.
items.description: Use the aria-describedby attribute to associate the error message with the corresponding input element.
items.whyItMatters: Without aria-describedby, screen readers announce the input name but silently skip the adjacent error message.
items.good: Email is required

items.title: Make sure that error, warning, and success states are not visually communicated by just color.
items.description: Color is not perceived by all users; additional indicators ensure everyone receives the same information.
items.whyItMatters: 8% of men have red-green colorblindness — they cannot distinguish red "error" from green "success" states.
items.good: ⚠ Error: invalid email

items.title: Make sure that media does not autoplay.
items.description: Automatically playing audio can interfere with screen readers.
items.whyItMatters: Autoplaying audio overrides screen reader output — users who navigate by listening cannot hear their AT.

items.title: Ensure that media controls use appropriate markup.
items.description: Use semantic elements for media controls: button for mute/play, input type=range for volume.
items.whyItMatters: Div-based media controls are not keyboard-operable and do not communicate state changes to screen readers.
items.good:
<button aria-pressed="false" onclick="mute()">Mute</button>
<input type="range" min="0" max="100" aria-label="Volume">

items.title: Check to see that all media can be paused.
items.description: Providing a pause mechanism is important for those who may find movement distracting.
items.whyItMatters: Constantly moving media distracts users with attention disorders and can interfere with screen reader output.
items.good:
<video src="hero.mp4">
  <!-- Pause control must be provided -->
</video>
<button onclick="togglePause()">Pause video</button>

items.title: Confirm the presence of captions.
items.description: Do all videos have caption tracks?
items.whyItMatters: Deaf and hard-of-hearing users rely on captions to access spoken content in videos.
items.good:
<video controls src="talk.mp4">
  <track kind="captions" srclang="en" src="talk.vtt" default>
</video>

items.title: Remove seizure triggers.
items.description: Rapidly flashing content can trigger seizures in people with photosensitive epilepsy.
items.whyItMatters: Content flashing more than 3 times/second can trigger seizures in people with photosensitive epilepsy.
items.bad:
/* Animation flashing at 5Hz — exceeds 3 flash/sec limit */
@keyframes flash { 0%,50% { opacity:1; } 51%,100% { opacity:0; } }
items.good: /* Keep animations below 3 flashes per second */

items.title: Confirm that transcripts are available.
items.description: Do all podcasts and audio-only content have a text transcript?
items.whyItMatters: Deaf users and those in sound-sensitive environments rely on transcripts to access audio content.

items.title: Check your content in specialized browsing modes.
items.description: Browsing modes such as Windows High Contrast Mode, Night Mode, and grayscale can affect your design.
items.whyItMatters: CSS gradient backgrounds and shadow-only borders disappear in Windows High Contrast Mode, making buttons invisible.
items.good: button { background-color: ButtonFace; color: ButtonText; border: 1px solid currentColor; }

items.title: Increase text size to 200%.
items.description: Content or functionality should not be lost when text is resized up to 200% of its original size.
items.whyItMatters: Fixed-height containers clip text when users increase font size, hiding content from low-vision users.

items.title: Double-check that good proximity between content is maintained.
items.description: Labels and their form controls should be visually adjacent to each other.
items.whyItMatters: Visual proximity helps users with cognitive disabilities associate labels with inputs, and is critical for screen magnification users.
items.bad: /* Label and input are separated by large whitespace */
items.good:
<label for="email">Email</label>
<input id="email" type="email"> <!-- directly below label */

items.title: Make sure color isn't the only way information is conveyed.
items.description: Do not rely on color alone to convey information to users.
items.whyItMatters: 8% of men have red-green colorblindness — color-only indicators are invisible to them.

items.title: Make sure instructions are not visual or audio-only.
items.description: Instructions that are purely sensory (e.g. "the round button") do not make sense to all users.
items.whyItMatters: Instructions that rely on shape, color, or position exclude blind users who cannot perceive those visual characteristics.
items.bad: Click the green button on the right to continue.
items.good: Click the green "Continue" button to proceed to the next step.

items.title: Use a simple, straightforward, and consistent layout.
items.description: A simple layout supports cognitive accessibility and makes responsive design more achievable.
items.whyItMatters: Complex, inconsistent layouts increase cognitive load and break when text is zoomed or windows are narrowed.
items.bad: /* Complex multi-column layout that breaks at 320px */

items.title: Ensure animations are subtle and do not flash too much.
items.description: Flashing or very rapid animations can be a trigger for people with photosensitive epilepsy.
items.whyItMatters: Rapid, large-scale animations can trigger vestibular disorders and photosensitive epilepsy seizures.
items.bad: .spinner { animation: spin 0.1s linear infinite; } /* too fast */

items.title: Provide a mechanism to pause background video.
items.description: Ensure that media that starts playing automatically can also be paused.
items.whyItMatters: Moving background content distracts users with attention disorders and can interfere with screen reader output.
items.good:
<video autoplay loop muted src="hero.mp4"></video>
<button onclick="togglePause()" aria-pressed="false">Pause background video</button>

items.title: Make sure all animation obeys the prefers-reduced-motion media query.
items.description: Remove animations when the "Reduce motion" accessibility setting is activated.
items.whyItMatters: Users with vestibular disorders experience nausea and dizziness from animations — prefers-reduced-motion lets them opt out.
items.bad: .fade { animation: fadeIn 0.3s ease; } /* no motion check */

items.title: Check the contrast for all normal-sized text.
items.description: Normal text must have a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 against its background.
items.whyItMatters: Low-contrast text is unreadable for people with low vision, color deficiencies, or in bright lighting.

items.title: Check the contrast for all large-sized text.
items.description: Large-scale text (≥18px or ≥14px bold) must have a contrast ratio of at least 3:1.
items.whyItMatters: Large text needs at least 3:1 contrast — still too low can make headings illegible for low-vision users.

items.title: Check the contrast for all icons.
items.description: Icons used as UI controls or conveying information need at least 3:1 contrast against their background.
items.whyItMatters: Low-contrast icons are invisible to low-vision users and in bright sunlight on mobile screens.

items.title: Check the contrast of borders for input elements.
items.description: Input borders must be distinguishable from their background at 3:1 contrast ratio.
items.whyItMatters: Invisible input borders make it impossible for low-vision users to identify where form fields are.

items.title: Check text that overlaps images or video.
items.description: When text is placed on top of images or video, ensure sufficient contrast by using a scrim.
items.whyItMatters: Busy photo backgrounds can make overlaid text unreadable, especially for low-vision users.
items.bad: /* White text directly on busy photo — unpredictable contrast */

items.title: Check custom ::selection colors.
items.description: Custom text selection colors must still maintain sufficient contrast.
items.whyItMatters: Custom ::selection styles using light colors on light backgrounds make selected text invisible.

items.title: Check that the site can be rotated to any orientation.
items.description: Make sure your site is not restricted to only landscape or portrait orientations.
items.whyItMatters: Some wheelchair users have their device mounted in a fixed orientation — locking orientation makes the site unusable for them.
items.good: /* No orientation lock — content adapts to both portrait and landscape */

items.title: Remove horizontal scrolling.
items.description: Ensure content does not require horizontal scrolling.
items.whyItMatters: Horizontal scrolling on mobile is disorienting and prevents magnification users from reading content in a single flow.
items.bad: .container { width: 1200px; } /* fixed width wider than viewport */

items.title: Ensure that button and link icons can be activated with ease.
items.description: Make sure touch controls are usable by a wide range of hand and stylus sizes.
items.whyItMatters: Small touch targets cause users with motor impairments or tremors to frequently activate the wrong element.
items.good: button { min-width: 44px; min-height: 44px; } /* WCAG AAA minimum */

items.title: Ensure sufficient space between interactive items in order to provide a scroll area.
items.description: No space between buttons makes it harder for users with physical disabilities to scroll past without activating something.
items.whyItMatters: Dense interactive elements make it impossible for users with tremors to scroll past them on touch screens.
items.bad: .button-list button { margin: 0; } /* no gap between buttons */
